root_text.sn22.122:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā ca sāriputto āyasmā ca mahākoṭṭhiko bārāṇasiyaṁ viharanti isipatane migadāye.
root_text.sn22.122:1.2: Atha kho āyasmā mahākoṭṭhiko sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yenāyasmā sāriputto tenupasaṅkami …pe… etadavoca:
root_text.sn22.122:1.3: “sīlavatāvuso, sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasikātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.122:1.4: “Sīlavatāvuso, koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:1.7: Sīlavatāvuso, koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:1.8: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati yaṁ sīlavā bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto sotāpattiphalaṁ sacchikareyyā”ti.
root_text.sn22.122:2.1: “Sotāpannena panāvuso sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.122:2.2: “Sotāpannenapi kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:2.3: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati yaṁ sotāpanno bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto sakadāgāmiphalaṁ sacchikareyyā”ti.
root_text.sn22.122:3.1: “Sakadāgāminā panāvuso sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.122:3.2: “Sakadāgāmināpi kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:3.3: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati yaṁ sakadāgāmī bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto anāgāmiphalaṁ sacchikareyyā”ti.
root_text.sn22.122:4.1: “Anāgāminā panāvuso sāriputta, bhikkhunā katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.122:4.2: “Anāgāmināpi kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, bhikkhunā ime pañcupādānakkhandhā aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:4.3: Ṭhānaṁ kho panetaṁ, āvuso, vijjati yaṁ anāgāmī bhikkhu ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato …pe… anattato yoniso manasi karonto arahattaṁ sacchikareyyā”ti.
root_text.sn22.122:5.1: “Arahatā panāvuso sāriputta, katame dhammā yoniso manasi kātabbā”ti?
root_text.sn22.122:5.2: “Arahatāpi kho, āvuso koṭṭhika, ime pañcupādānakkhandhe aniccato dukkhato rogato gaṇḍato sallato aghato ābādhato parato palokato suññato anattato yoniso manasi kātabbā.
root_text.sn22.122:5.3: Natthi, khvāvuso, arahato uttari karaṇīyaṁ katassa vā paticayo;
root_text.sn22.122:5.4: api ca ime dhammā bhāvitā bahulīkatā diṭṭhadhammasukhavihārāya ceva saṁvattanti satisampajaññāya cā”ti.

translation_text.sn22.122:1.1: At one time Venerable Sāriputta and Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita were staying near Varanasi, in the deer park at Isipatana.
translation_text.sn22.122:1.2: Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Mahākoṭṭhita came out of retreat, went to Venerable Sāriputta, and said:
translation_text.sn22.122:1.3: “Reverend Sāriputta, what things should an ethical mendicant rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.122:1.4: “Reverend Koṭṭhita, an ethical mendicant should rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:1.6: That is, the grasping aggregates of form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.122:1.7: An ethical mendicant should rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:1.8: It’s possible that an ethical mendicant who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of stream-entry.”
translation_text.sn22.122:2.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant stream-enterer rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.122:2.2: “A mendicant stream-enterer should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:2.3: It’s possible that a mendicant stream-enterer who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of once-return.”
translation_text.sn22.122:3.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant once-returner rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.122:3.2: “A mendicant once-returner should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:3.3: It’s possible that a mendicant once-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize the fruit of non-return.”
translation_text.sn22.122:4.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a mendicant non-returner rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.122:4.2: “A mendicant non-returner should also rationally apply the mind to these five grasping aggregates as impermanent … as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:4.3: It’s possible that a mendicant non-returner who rationally applies the mind to the five grasping aggregates will realize perfection.”
translation_text.sn22.122:5.1: “But Reverend Sāriputta, what things should a perfected one rationally apply the mind to?”
translation_text.sn22.122:5.2: “Reverend Koṭṭhita, a perfected one should also rationally apply the mind to the five grasping aggregates as impermanent, as suffering, as diseased, as a boil, as a dart, as gloom, as an affliction, as alien, as breaking apart, as empty, as not-self.
translation_text.sn22.122:5.3: A perfected one has nothing more to do, and nothing that needs improvement.
translation_text.sn22.122:5.4: Still, these things, when developed and cultivated, lead to blissful meditation in this life, and also to mindfulness and situational awareness.”
